scenario: A FEEL expression must check if a variable is of type list vs context vs scalar -- useful for polymorphic handling. Does FEEL have an instance of operator for type checking?
question: Does FEEL have an instance of operator for type checking?

options.text: Use the typeof() function to check FEEL value types at runtime -- this function name is invented and has no entry in the Camunda FEEL built-in function library. The FEEL specification and Camunda's implementation use the instance of infix operator for runtime type checks. Syntax is value instance of <type-name> returning a boolean. Available type names include boolean, number, string, date, time, list, context, function, and Any.

FEEL has no mechanism for checking the type of a value at runtime -- this contradicts the documented FEEL language capabilities. md:184-221. This operator enables polymorphic handling of variables that may hold different value types depending on context. Type checking is particularly useful when working with data from external systems that may send either scalar values or list structures.

md:184-221. Syntax: value instance of <type>. Available type names (verbatim from :188-200): boolean, number, string, date, time, date and time, days and time duration, years and months duration, list, context, function, Any (use Any to check non-null per :201). name else "unknown". Useful for polymorphic data handling.

The instance of operator works only for primitive types like number, string, and boolean -- this understates the full type coverage. md:184-221, the operator supports all documented FEEL type names: scalars (boolean, number, string), temporals (date, time, date and time, days and time duration, years and months duration), collections (list, context), functions, and the special Any sentinel for non-null checks. Polymorphic FEEL expressions can branch on list vs context vs scalar types.

a.text: Incorrect. FEEL provides instance of for runtime type checking. Syntax: value instance of . Returns boolean. Supported types per FEEL spec: Scalars: number, string, boolean. Temporals: date, time, date and time, days and time duration, years and months duration. Collections: list, context (object), range. Functions: function. Null check: value = null (not instance of null; null is the value, not a type). Per feel-boolean-expressions.md:184-221, the full type name list is: Scalars: boolean, number, string. Temporals: date, time, date and time, days and time duration, years and months duration. Collections: list, context. Functions: function. Any (per :201 "Use the type Any to check if the value is not null"). Note: there is no range or null type name in the documented list -- null-checks use value = null or value instance of Any (the latter returns false for null per :213-214). Use cases: Polymorphic data: variable may be a single item OR a list; check before processing. Defensive coding: validate variable shape before using. Type narrowing: in complex FEEL, narrow type before accessing fields. Verbatim doc examples (:203-221): ` 1 instance of number // true 1 instance of string // false 1 instance of Any // true null instance of Any // false duration("P3M") instance of years and months duration // true duration("PT4H") instance of days and time duration // true `

b.text: Incorrect. instance of operator, not function.
